definition: Spiritual torpor; the inability to feel; the deadening of moral and emotional responsiveness.
lost: Named the specific sin of not caring — acedia is distinct from sadness because sadness still feels, and from laziness because laziness still wants.
usage: It was not grief that had taken him from his work but something older and more resistant: a pure acedia.

definition: Scorched, dried out; in humoral medicine, a condition in which the blood has been burnt by an internal heat, producing melancholy, brittleness, a characteristic aridity of spirit.
lost: Nothing in modern English names the specific exhaustion that comes from burning from the inside — the feeling of having been dried out by your own heat.
usage: She was adust in a way that sleep no longer touched.

definition: A fever accompanied by shivering fits; malaria in its cyclical form; the cold inside the heat of illness.
lost: Named the paradox of the fevered body — ague is burning from inside while shaking from cold, the body in contradiction with itself.
usage: He had taken an ague in the marshes that returned every third day regardless of the season, until finally it didn't.

definition: A direction of the compass; the quarter from which the wind blows; to direct or guide toward a point.
lost: Named direction as something the wind comes from rather than something you travel toward — airt understood that weather has addresses.
usage: He read the airt of the wind before he launched, and then ignored it, which was the last mistake he made.

definition: Eternally bright, unfading — of a color or quality that does not diminish with time; named for the amaranth flower, which was believed to be immortal.
lost: "Eternal" describes duration. Amaranthine describes condition — the refusal to fade is active, ongoing.
usage: The grief was amaranthine; it did not soften with the years.

definition: A cupboard or recess in a wall for storing food or utensils; also: a niche in a church wall for sacred vessels.
lost: Named the storage space that had both sacred and domestic uses — the ambry was wherever you kept the things that mattered, whether bread or chalice.
usage: The ambry in the kitchen wall held the salt and the good knives and a small flask of something that had been there since before anyone could remember.

definition: Shortly; at once; very soon — a word that holds two contradictory senses: immediately and in a little while.
lost: Named imminence without precision — anon is a promise of soon that carries no commitment to exactly when, which is either honest or evasive depending on who says it.
usage: I will be with you anon, she said, which covered anything from one minute to an hour and everyone understood.

definition: A terse, pointed, memorable saying; a maxim that contains more than its word count suggests.
lost: Named the maxim with the implication that it cuts — an apophthegm is a saying you remember because it drew blood.
usage: He was known for his apophthegms; people collected them the way others collect wounds.

definition: Having the power to ward off evil or bad luck; designed to avert malevolent forces; the quality of objects kept for protection.
lost: Named a function that objects can perform — apotropaic things are not decorative or useful in any material sense but are kept because they are believed to push something bad away.
usage: The horseshoe above the door was apotropaic rather than decorative, which she knew because it had been there before she was born and would stay there after she was gone.

definition: The specific warmth of winter sunlight — warmth that touches the skin without persuading the season, that promises nothing and yet is welcome for exactly what it is.
lost: "Winter sunshine" is meteorological. Apricity names the particular quality: warmth without conviction.
usage: She turned her face to the apricity and did not open her eyes.

definition: Across the width of a ship; at right angles to the fore-and-aft line; also: across the path of something, in opposition to its direction.
lost: Named the direction that cuts against — athwart is the transverse axis of a vessel but also the adversarial direction, the angle that opposes. A ship lying athwart the channel is not moving with it but blocking it.
usage: The wreck lay athwart the fairway, and there was no passage to either side that had enough water for them.

definition: Of a melancholy and gloomy temperament; afflicted with black bile; constitutionally dark in mood and outlook.
lost: Named the temperament that the humoral system understood as a physical condition — atrabilious people were not choosing their gloom, it was in their blood, which was either more or less forgiving than the modern understanding.
usage: He grew atrabilious in autumn as reliably as the leaves changed, and his family adjusted their plans accordingly.

definition: The legal extinction of a person's civil rights following a death sentence for treason or felony; the corruption of blood that barred a traitor's heirs from inheriting.
lost: Named the punishment that outlasted the person — attainder extended the sentence into the next generation, punishing children for their parent's crime in a way modern law refuses.
usage: The attainder was reversed by the next reign, which restored the estate to the son but could not restore the twenty years.

definition: The reading of omens from natural signs — birds, weather, chance events — and by extension, any portent or sign; the practice of treating the world as legible, as actively communicating.
lost: "Omen" is passive — a thing that exists. Augury assumes the world is sending information, and that attention is the act of receiving it.
usage: He had learned augury young and never entirely stopped practicing it.

definition: Golden in color or quality; of a gilded light; also: ornate in literary style.
lost: Named the specific register of gold-light, which is warmer and heavier than yellow and more earned than bright.
usage: An aureate afternoon light lay across the city and made even the industrial quarter seem momentarily forgiven.

definition: A circle of light surrounding a holy figure; a halo; any luminous ring or crown of radiance around a source of light.
lost: Named the light that announces holiness rather than merely illuminates — an aureole is light as attribute, light as argument, which is a different thing from light as condition.
usage: The mist had caught the lantern light and given it an aureole that made the figure at the gate look briefly like something other than what he was.

definition: An omen read from the flight of birds; any patronage or protective guidance.
lost: The modern word collapsed into its metaphor — we kept 'under the auspices of' but lost the bird, and the bird was doing the work.
usage: They set out under good auspice: three herons flying north, and no clouds past the ridge.

definition: To affirm or assert positively; to vouch for as true; to maintain under challenge.
lost: Named assertion with the implication of standing behind it — to avouch something was to put yourself in the sentence, to make the statement personal.
usage: She avouched it was true, which meant the question moved from evidence to character.

definition: Evil, woe, destruction — not as a moral judgment but as a force; the active quality of harm, the thing in things that unmakes. Used in compounds: baleful, balefire.
lost: "Evil" is moral. Bale is elemental — not a judgment but a property, the way cold is a property of winter.
usage: Whatever had passed through here, it had left its bale in the soil.

definition: A large open-air fire; a signal fire lit on a hilltop to communicate across distance; the fire that means something is happening.
lost: Named fire as communication — a balefire was not warmth or cooking but a sentence, visible across miles, that said something had changed.
usage: The balefires on the coastal headlands were lit before the ships had cleared the horizon, and the news arrived before the ships did.

definition: Bound by a debt of gratitude; owing something to someone in a way that outlasts the original favor.
lost: Named the social debt that doesn't appear on ledgers — beholden is the obligation you carry in how you speak about someone.
usage: She was beholden to no one, which she had arranged deliberately and at some cost.

definition: An old woman; originally grandmother (belle dame); later: a hag, a witch, a fearsome old woman whose age has given her a power that frightens.
lost: Traced the trajectory from grandmother to witch — beldam shows the cultural anxiety about older women, the way age and female knowledge became threatening over the same century the word changed meaning.
usage: A beldam at the crossroads who claimed to know which road was passable and whose advice they eventually decided was worth the coin she asked.

definition: Perhaps, probably — but with a quality of resigned expectation rather than mere uncertainty. The speaker already suspects the answer.
lost: "Maybe" is open. Belike suggests you already know, and are preparing yourself.
usage: "Belike she's heard about the rest of them," he said, and did not finish the thought.

definition: The deep, resonant cry of a deer at rut; the call that carries across valleys and announces the season's urgency.
lost: Named the specific voice of autumn — belling is a sound that tells you the year is turning, that the stag is making his claim on time as well as territory.
usage: They heard the belling before they saw him: a sound that seemed to belong to the hillside itself.

definition: To shower or drench thoroughly; to be rained upon comprehensively; to receive the full attention of bad weather.
lost: Named the soaking as something done to you with intent — to be beshowered is to be singled out by rain, not merely caught in it.
usage: He arrived beshowered and in no mood to hear that the meeting had been moved.

definition: To curse; to invoke ill upon; a mild but heartfelt imprecation — beshrew me was a common self-deprecating oath.
lost: Named the light curse — beshrew was oaths for everyday frustration, the swearing that acknowledged wrong without calling down serious consequence.
usage: Beshrew the man, she said, and meant it, and then got on with what needed doing.

definition: To speak for; to engage or order in advance; also: to be evidence of, to indicate clearly.
lost: Carried two meanings that share an underlying logic — to bespeak something is either to claim it with words or to have it claimed by appearance, which are structurally the same act.
usage: His manner bespoke a familiarity with authority that his clothes did not quite support.

definition: In good time; early; before it was too late — carrying the implication that the earliness was deliberate, that the choice to act before the hour was a considered one.
lost: "Early" is positional. Betimes carries intention — and sometimes relief that the intention was followed.
usage: They left betimes, while the road was still quiet and the border guards still sleeping.

definition: The zone of a ship's hull at the waterline, alternately exposed and submerged as she rolls; struck there, a vessel takes water with every wave. To hit something between wind and water is to find the fatal vulnerability.
lost: Named the most dangerous point on any hull — not the bilge, not the bow, but the waterline, where the sea enters with the ship's own motion. A shot between wind and water killed ships slowly and certainly. The phrase kept its meaning — a fatal blow at the vulnerable point — long after the wooden warships that gave it currency were gone.
usage: The comment landed between wind and water, which was not his intent but was the effect, and he watched her expression change before she recovered.

definition: To reveal or betray inadvertently; to expose what was hidden, not by intention but by accident or appearance.
lost: Named the unintended revelation — bewray is the exposure that happens without malice, the tell that the body gives when the mouth is silent.
usage: His accent bewrayed him before he had said three sentences, and after that the conversation was different.

definition: A finely tempered sword from Bilbao; a blade known for its flexibility and edge; also: a bar with sliding shackles used to confine prisoners.
lost: Named the sword by its city of origin, as the best blades always were — a bilbo was a specific quality of steel, a reputation made metal.
usage: He carried a bilbo he had taken at Cadiz and never found its equal in thirty years of looking.

definition: A gleam of sunshine breaking through cloud; a brief, hopeful clearing in overcast sky.
lost: Named the exact moment of weather reversing — blenk is sunshine not as condition but as event, the instant the grey gives way.
usage: A blenk in the afternoon raised everyone's spirits briefly before the clouds closed again.

definition: To blow in strong, irregular gusts; wind that makes noise and movement without the steadiness of a true gale.
lost: Named wind that is performing rather than committed — bluster is wind that makes a great deal of fuss without the sustained force that would make it genuinely dangerous.
usage: A blustering day that threw handfuls of rain against the windows without managing proper precipitation.

definition: A favor formally requested; a granted petition; a gift given in response to a specific ask.
lost: We kept boon as 'windfall' but lost the formal structure — a boon was asked, not merely received.
usage: She would grant him one boon, she said, and he should choose it carefully.

definition: To quarrel noisily about trifles; petty argument at volume; the kind of dispute that everyone in the room can hear and no one benefits from.
lost: Named the quarrel that isn't worth having but is being had regardless — brabble is argument stripped of stakes, which makes the noise worse.
usage: The brabble over who sat where had been going for ten minutes and showed no sign of resolving in either direction.

definition: A sudden brief shower of rain or hail; a squall that arrives and passes before you have time to shelter.
lost: Named the shower as a discrete event with arrival and departure — a brash is weather with a beginning and end, which most weather refuses to have.
usage: They were caught in a brash on the hillside and were soaked and dry again before they reached the valley.

definition: A badger; the old name that predates the Norse-derived 'badger' and carries more of the animal's stubbornness.
lost: Brock survived in dialect and in the names of fields that once contained setts — the word carries the animal's characteristic refusal to be moved.
usage: Something had been into the vegetable garden: a brock, from the depth and purpose of the digging.

definition: A stag in its second year, with its first unbranched antler spike; the deer between fawn and adult, claiming horns before earning them.
lost: Named the adolescent stag in its awkward year — the brocket carries its first antlers with the specific self-consciousness of something that has just become dangerous.
usage: A brocket at the edge of the wood, close enough to the path that they slowed to watch it decide whether to flee.

definition: A sound, report, or rumor; to spread a report; the sound of gossip traveling.
lost: Held noise and rumor as the same force — bruit understood that information and sound both travel by air.
usage: The bruit of the execution reached the port before the tide turned.

definition: Mist or fog; a cold, heavy vapour that clings to valleys and water at dusk; the weather of in-between light.
lost: Named fog with more weight than mist allows — brume is fog that has settled in and intends to stay, fog with mass.
usage: A brume lay in the hollows all morning, and the hilltops appeared to float on it like islands.

definition: To laugh loudly, harshly, and without restraint; to laugh in a way that fills a room whether welcome or not.
lost: Named the laugh that the laugher cannot control — not joyful, but released.
usage: He cachinnated at the wrong moment and the interview was effectively over.

definition: A despicable coward; a wretched prisoner; a person base enough in character to have forfeited the right to sympathy.
lost: Named cowardice at its lowest register — a caitiff is not merely afraid but contemptibly so, the kind of coward who makes others feel their own fear is a different thing entirely.
usage: He had thought himself capable of courage; the moment proved him a caitiff, and he did not forget it.

definition: A device with four metal spikes arranged so one always points upward; scattered on ground to lame horses and men; cruelty made geometric.
lost: Named a weapon that required no wielder — a caltrop works without anyone present, which is either ingenious or a specific kind of dishonesty depending on your view of war.
usage: They found the caltrops in the ford at the cost of two horses and one man, which was the point.

definition: A false and malicious accusation; deliberate misrepresentation designed to damage a reputation.
lost: Named deliberate dishonesty without using the word 'lie' — calumny is a lie with a specific target and intent.
usage: The calumny had been in circulation for three years before anyone thought to check its source.

definition: Glowing with white heat; incandescent; the state of metal or mineral heated to the point where it emits light.
lost: Named the specific temperature of light — candent is the heat that has become visible, the moment at which matter stops being merely hot and begins to radiate.
usage: The forge was candent by noon; the smith worked by the quality of the light his metal gave off rather than by any clock.

definition: To heel a ship onto her side for cleaning and repair; to expose the underwater hull by tipping the vessel until the keel is accessible. The careened ship is helpless, vulnerable, fully committed.
lost: The modern sense — careening wildly — came from watching a ship careen, which moves fast and without control across the beach as gravity takes her over. The origin is a ship deliberately made helpless for maintenance, which is a different and more interesting thing.
usage: They careened her on the beach at low water and worked through the tide to burn the weed off before she floated again.

definition: To find fault persistently and querulously; to complain about small things with the energy usually reserved for large ones.
lost: Named the complaint that has become a habit — carp is fault-finding as a reflex, the person who has organised their attention around what is wrong.
usage: She carped through the entire performance and then said she had enjoyed herself, and seemed to believe both things.

definition: A group of badgers; the collective noun for badgers inhabiting a shared sett; the social unit of that most solitary-seeming of animals.
lost: Named the collective with a precision that reveals something — badgers are grouped when they come together underground, not when seen above it, which means the cete is a social fact most people never witness.
usage: The cete had been in that bank for longer than anyone could say; the sett was ancient and mapped on documents no one had looked at.

definition: Distress and embarrassment caused by failure or humiliation; the specific discomfort of being shown to be wrong in front of others.
lost: Named the compound of shame and disappointment — chagrin is not merely embarrassment but the embarrassment of someone who thought better of themselves, which is a more precise wound.
usage: To his chagrin, the door he had confidently declared locked was simply stiff.

definition: Of or relating to dead bodies and the places where they accumulate; having the matter-of-fact quality of a house of bones — not gothic horror but practical mortality.
lost: We have euphemisms. Charnel is the word for when you're past them.
usage: The smell was charnel and old; whatever had died here had been dying for years.

definition: The yellow bile; anger understood as a physical substance; hot-tempered irascibility read as a condition of the blood.
lost: Named anger as a bodily fluid rather than an emotion — choler understood that bad temper was something you could adjust, a medical problem as well as a moral one.
usage: His choler rose at every small setback, which those around him had learned to absorb without responding until it had run its course.

definition: A red-billed, red-legged crow of coastal cliffs and uplands; a bird associated with Cornwall, Wales, and the edges of things.
lost: Named a bird that lives at margins — the chough inhabits cliff faces and thin soils, places where the land runs out, and the word has that quality of standing at an edge.
usage: A pair of choughs worked the cliff face below them, their calls carrying up on the updraft like questions.

definition: A free peasant of the lowest rank; a rough, rude person; one who lacks the courtesy that rank imposes.
lost: Named both a legal class and a character type — the churl was the lowest free man, and the word understood that low birth and rough manners were expected to coincide.
usage: He had risen far enough to be addressed as sir, but there was something churl in his manner that his money had not quite covered.

definition: A loud, resonant, prolonged ringing; the sound of metal struck repeatedly; noise that reverberates and accumulates.
lost: Named metallic sound at its maximum — clangour is not a single strike but the accumulated resonance of many, sound become an environment.
usage: The clangour from the smithy carried across the town all morning, marking the hours as reliably as the church bell.

definition: To call or name a thing; to give it its true designation. More formal than "call" — implying the act of naming carries consequence.
lost: "Call" is casual. Clepe implies the name, once given, binds both parties.
usage: By what name do they clepe this road in the old maps?

definition: To converse privately; to conspire or intrigue through whispered talk; to flatter with intent.
lost: Named conversation with a motive — collogue implies the talk has a purpose being kept from the room.
usage: He collogued with the steward for an hour before the meeting, and the result showed.

definition: Pleasing in appearance; well-proportioned; attractive without being exceptional.
lost: Named the register of beauty that doesn't make demands — comely is attractive within a human scale, not beyond it.
usage: She was comely in the way of people who have aged without argument, in good proportion with themselves.

definition: The last of the canonical hours; the night prayer that closes the day's liturgical cycle; the moment when the religious day is formally done.
lost: Named the end of the day as a ritual threshold — compline marked the hour when the day was concluded, not merely finished, which is a distinction worth having.
usage: After compline the monks kept silence until prime, which meant the night had a texture the secular world had abandoned.

definition: The legal practice of clearing oneself of a charge by oath-helpers who swear to one's innocence.
lost: Named a legal institution built on the social weight of a person's standing — compurgation assumed that who will swear for you is evidence of who you are.
usage: He called for compurgation, and twelve men of the parish stood and swore, and it was enough.

definition: A rabbit, especially a tame or kept one; before 'rabbit' displaced it, this was the word for the creature at all ages.
lost: Rabbit originally meant only the young of the coney; when rabbit expanded to cover all ages, coney retreated into dialect and then silence.
usage: The coney warrens on the hillside were property of the manor, and taking one carried the same penalty as taking a deer.

definition: Of the same blood; related by birth; sharing a common ancestor.
lost: Named blood relation as a legal and moral category, not merely a biological fact — consanguinity created obligations.
usage: They were consanguine, which the law treated as a fact and they treated as a complication.

definition: A horse dealer; a trader in cattle or livestock; one who deals and barters, especially with sharp practice implied.
lost: Named a specific commercial type that required its own word — the coper was not merely a seller but one who knew every trick the animal could hide.
usage: He bought the horse from a coper at the Michaelmas fair and spent the following winter regretting it.

definition: Originally: sheathed below the waterline in copper plate, which resisted the shipworm that devoured unprotected timber; hence: reliable, sound, guaranteed. The phrase outlived the technology.
lost: The shipworm Teredo navalis could destroy an unprotected hull in months; copper sheathing stopped it and changed naval warfare. A copper-bottomed ship could stay at sea longer and in warmer waters. The guarantee the phrase still implies was once literal — copper-bottomed meant the ship would not rot beneath you.
usage: He described it as a copper-bottomed investment, not knowing that the phrase once meant a ship that would not dissolve in tropical water.

definition: A raven or crow; in Scottish ballads, the carrion crow that arrives at the battlefield before the burial party.
lost: Named the crow with the weight of what crows attended — a corbie in a ballad is not merely a bird but an announcement of what the field contains.
usage: Three corbies settled on the gate before dawn, which the old woman said was sufficient to cancel the journey.

definition: A phosphorescent glow seen moving over graveyards or marshes at night; the light believed to mark where the dead lay or would soon lie.
lost: Named a natural phenomenon as a supernatural one — corpse-light was bioluminescence before that word existed, and the name understood that light in a wrong place carries information.
usage: The sexton had seen corpse-lights three times in twenty years; all three times, a grave was dug within the fortnight.

definition: To flash and sparkle; to throw off light in intermittent bursts; to glitter in motion.
lost: Gave motion to light — coruscate is light doing something, not just being somewhere.
usage: The harbor coruscated with the broken reflections of a thousand lanterns.

definition: A solemn, binding agreement; a contract that carries moral or spiritual weight beyond its legal terms.
lost: Named the agreement that means more than its clauses — a covenant is not voided by a technicality because it was never merely technical.
usage: Their arrangement was not a contract; it was a covenant, and that distinction meant everything when it was tested.

definition: A dry crackling or rattling sound — the sound of things that have lost their moisture, their flexibility; the sound of brittle things under pressure, or of fire consuming paper.
lost: "Crackling" is onomatopoeic but imprecise. Crepitation specifies the quality — dry, rapid, repetitive, the sound of material yielding.
usage: The crepitation of the burning papers was the only sound in the room for a long time.

definition: The brief, specific moment of twilight — neither day nor night, the threshold state. A temporal condition of radical uncertainty.
lost: "Dusk" is the result. Crepuscule is the event, with all its instability intact.
usage: He always made his approach at crepuscule, when shapes were still uncertain.

definition: Originally: to cut the anchor cable rather than weigh anchor, and run before the wind — abandoning the anchor to gain speed immediately. Speed bought by sacrifice of equipment and ground.
lost: Weighing anchor was slow, careful work. In emergency — a surprise attack, a sudden storm, a lee shore — there was no time for it. You cut the cable and lost the anchor and ran. The phrase kept its meaning of hasty abandonment while losing the specific geometry: the anchor left on the bottom, the frayed cable streaming from the hawse.
usage: They cut and ran at midnight, leaving the anchorage without lights or signal, the cable end trailing in the water behind them.

definition: A light drizzle; a fine mist of rain so thin it barely constitutes weather, arriving more as a condition than an event.
lost: Named rain at its most ambiguous — dag is what you argue about whether to call rain at all, the moisture that accrues without ever committing.
usage: A dag had been falling all morning, too light to matter and too persistent to ignore.

definition: A small, insignificant person; a pert little fellow; originally a small coin of negligible value.
lost: Named insignificance with a specific contempt — a dandiprat is not merely small but trivially so, worth about threepence.
usage: Some dandiprat from the counting house had been given the contract, and no one could explain why.

definition: To grow dark; to become obscure; to lie hidden in the dark.
lost: We have no verb for darkness increasing — 'darkle' gave the process agency that 'getting darker' refuses.
usage: The coast darkling behind them, they rowed toward the open sea.

definition: A jackdaw; by extension, a chattering, thieving simpleton — the bird lent its character to the insult.
lost: Named the bird and the person it resembled in one syllable — daw understood that jackdaws and certain people share an acquisitiveness and a noise.
usage: He was a daw in a magistrate's coat; everyone knew it, and the knowledge changed nothing.

definition: A fixed porthole that admits light but cannot be opened; a thick glass port sealed into the hull; light without communication, illumination without the possibility of escape.
lost: Named the porthole that does not open — a deadlight is light made passive, the sea visible but untouchable, a window that is permanently a window and never a door. Below the waterline, deadlights were the only natural light, giving the lower decks a cold, submarine glow.
usage: The light through the deadlight was green and cold and moved with the water outside, which made the cabin feel like something submerged.

definition: The land attached to and worked directly for a lord's household; the portion of the manor kept for the lord rather than leased out.
lost: Named the land that fed power directly — the demesne was not rented, not worked by tenants, but managed for the household's own provision, which is a specific relationship between land and authority.
usage: The demesne farms were the richest ground in the parish and the first to be enclosed, which was noted and remembered.

definition: An object that caused a person's death, legally forfeit to the crown as an offering to God.
lost: Held objects morally accountable in a way modern law refuses — a deodand is a thing that owes a debt.
usage: The coroner declared the millwheel a deodand; it was sold and the price given to the church.

definition: A knight's great warhorse; a horse bred and trained for battle, led by the right hand into combat.
lost: Named the horse by its purpose rather than its breed — a destrier was a social role, a piece of equipment, an investment worth a knight's ransom.
usage: The destrier stood sixteen hands and had been trained to kick and bite; he was valued accordingly and treated with more care than most men.

definition: The devil was the lowest seam on a wooden ship's hull, where the hull met the keel; to pay a seam was to caulk it with oakum and tar. The devil was the hardest seam to reach, requiring a man to hang over the side. The full phrase was 'the devil to pay and no pitch hot' — an impossible job without the means to do it.
lost: The phrase lost its second half and both its meanings — the specific seam nobody wanted to caulk, and the absent materials. What remained was a vague sense of consequences, stripped of the original's precise despair: a man hanging over the side of a ship in cold water with no tar.
usage: If the cargo inspectors arrived before the manifest was complete, there would be the devil to pay, which he said without knowing what he was remembering.

definition: A mournful song sung at a funeral; any slow, sad piece of music; from the Latin 'dirige', the first word of a funeral antiphon.
lost: Named the lament as a specific musical form — a dirge has structure, tempo, purpose; it is grief organised into something that can be performed, which is a way of making it bearable.
usage: They sang a dirge that nobody present knew the words to, which meant they hummed it, which was more affecting.

definition: Without consolation; beyond the reach of comfort; grief so complete that nothing offered can touch it.
lost: Named the condition where help has run out — disconsolate is not merely sad but inconsolable, the state in which the usual remedies have been tried and have failed.
usage: She was disconsolate in a way that made her friends feel helpless, because they were.

definition: A stick for holding wool during spinning; by extension: the female side of a family; women's work as a category.
lost: Named a tool and made it a metaphor — the distaff side of the family is the maternal line, women's inheritance, which the word encoded as naturally as breathing and we have since stopped noticing.
usage: On the distaff side the family had money going back four generations, which the male line had spent with impressive consistency.

definition: The practice of seeking knowledge of the future through supernatural means; reading the world as if it were a text written for you.
lost: Named a practice that assumed the world was legible — divination required believing that events had meaning beyond themselves, that the flight of birds or the fall of lots was addressed to someone.
usage: She practiced divination with the same methodical care she applied to everything else, which made it either more or less convincing depending on your prior beliefs.

definition: A charitable distribution of food or money to the poor; one's portion or lot; grief and mourning — the word carried all three meanings.
lost: Named giving and receiving and sorrow in one word — dole understood that charity was connected to grief, that distribution happened at deaths and disasters, that your portion was also your fate.
usage: They gave dole at the gate every Friday, which brought the same faces every week and a few new ones when times worsened.

definition: Full of grief and misery; mournful in appearance or sound; the quality of a thing that makes you sad to look at it.
lost: Named sadness as something a thing can have as well as a person — a dolesome aspect is melancholy made visible, grief become an attribute of the world.
usage: The house had a dolesome air long after the family had gone that the new tenants could not quite dispel with furniture.

definition: Deep grief and sorrow; a heavy, sustained anguish that settles rather than passes.
lost: Named grief with a weight that sadness doesn't carry — dolour is grief become a condition, something you live in rather than something that happens to you.
usage: The dolour of the house was palpable before you entered it; you could feel it in the quality of the silence.

definition: A binding judgment; a legal decision; fate as something decreed rather than merely arriving.
lost: We kept doom but stripped its judicial precision — Old English doom was a verdict, not a catastrophe.
usage: The doom was read aloud in the square so that none could claim ignorance of it.

definition: To search for underground water or minerals using a divining rod; to seek hidden things through bodily sensitivity rather than reason.
lost: Named a form of knowing that bypasses the mind — dowsing assumes the body knows what the intellect can't reach, which is either superstition or phenomenology depending on your sympathies.
usage: He could dowse for water with a hazel fork with a reliability that three generations of his family had noticed and one generation of engineers had tried to disprove.

definition: Dull, overcast, drizzly, and bleak; the specific Scottish quality of a cold, grey, wet day that refuses to commit to anything as dramatic as a storm.
lost: Named the weather that isn't bad enough to complain about properly — dreich is the weather of low-grade persistent misery, the day that is simply grey all the way through with no relief and no drama.
usage: A dreich morning that made staying indoors feel like a moral position rather than a choice.

definition: The darker stage of twilight; the quality of dimness and shadow; also as a verb: to grow dark.
lost: We kept dusk but lost its sense as a quality that could apply to any light — dusk once named a kind of darkness that could fall on an interior, a face, a mood, not just on the sky.
usage: The room dusked as the afternoon wore on; she did not light a candle, and eventually she could not see to read.

definition: Radiant; pouring forth light; shining brilliantly outward.
lost: Distinguished light as emanation from light as reflection — effulgent things produce light, they do not merely catch it.
usage: She walked into the room effulgent with a certainty that had no obvious cause.

definition: Shortly afterward; again, and soon — carrying the sense of inevitable return, of history's tendency to repeat with only cosmetic variation.
lost: "Soon" looks forward only. Eftsoons carries a memory of the last time.
usage: The river rose, and eftsoons the lower fields were lost again.

definition: Weird, uncanny, and otherworldly; having a quality of supernatural strangeness; the specific uncanniness of something from outside the known.
lost: Named the specific register of supernatural strangeness that weird has since absorbed — eldritch was stranger than strange, the quality of things that did not obey the same rules.
usage: An eldritch quality to the light at the edge of the wood that made them slow down before they had identified why.

definition: An ant; a creature of absolute purposefulness and no individual authority; also in Cornish dialect: a tourist or outsider.
lost: The Cornish usage is the sharpest — emmets are people who move in groups, purposefully, without understanding where they are, which is a precise description of both species.
usage: The emmets had found the honey in the larder before noon, and by midday there were too many to count.

definition: A formal speech or piece of writing in praise of a person or thing.
lost: Gave praise a structure and a cost — an encomium was a performance you prepared, not a comment you left.
usage: The encomium ran to forty minutes and the subject of it sat looking increasingly uncomfortable.

definition: To invest with a fief or estate; to bind someone to land and service by a formal grant.
lost: Named the act of binding land to people — enfeoffment created obligations that ran with the soil, not just with the individual.
usage: He enfeoffed his youngest son with the eastern pastures and the obligations that ran with them.

definition: A profound, listless tedium; the boredom of those who have everything and find it insufficient; spiritual vacancy dressed as sophistication.
lost: Named the specific boredom of surplus — ennui is not the boredom of the poor, who cannot afford it, but the boredom of those who have run out of wanting things, which is its own kind of poverty.
usage: An ennui had settled over the party by ten that no amount of music or drink could quite dispel.

definition: The condition of wandering without fixed purpose; the state of being one who travels by necessity of nature rather than plan — a recognized mode of being, with its own traditions and codes.
lost: "Wandering" is a gerund. Errantry is a vocation — a recognized state that carries its own dignity.
usage: Errantry had taught her more than any settled life could offer, but the cost was considerable.

definition: At a former time; belonging to the past — carrying the specific quality of something that was genuinely present and substantial and is now absent; a former state that was real.
lost: "Former" is administrative. Erstwhile implies the loss of the thing has changed the landscape around it.
usage: Her erstwhile certainty had been replaced by something more functional but less comforting.

definition: The time of evening; the close of the day understood as a daily threshold rather than a mere hour.
lost: Evening is a time; eventide is a quality of time — the word holds the day's ending as something that arrives and that one moves into, not just a clock reading.
usage: By eventide the harvest was in and the last light lay flat across the field in a way that made everyone stand still for a moment.

definition: The room or cupboard in a great house where ewers and basins for handwashing were kept; the department responsible for water and linen.
lost: Named a domestic institution that required its own space and its own staff — the ewery understood that hand-washing before and after meals was important enough to need dedicated management.
usage: The ewery boy had filled the basins before the guests arrived, which was noticed only when he had failed to do it, which was how most domestic work was noticed.

definition: Willing, content to, eager — but carrying a quality of resigned willingness; the acceptance of what must be done, stated as preference because preference and necessity have converged.
lost: "Willing" is neutral. Fain implies the willingness has been thought through, perhaps reluctantly.
usage: "I am fain to let it go," she said, and she meant it, though it had cost her considerable effort to mean it.

definition: A temple or shrine; a consecrated place; any building set apart for worship or sacred purpose.
lost: Gave the sacred place a word of its own that predates the Latin church vocabulary — fane is older than cathedral and carries a different weight.
usage: They found the fane in the wood long before they found the village it had served.

definition: Sworn loyalty to a lord or cause; the formal acknowledgment of a bond of obligation.
lost: Named loyalty as a witnessed, sworn act — fealty is loyalty that has been made public and therefore costs something.
usage: She had sworn fealty once, and she intended to be the kind of person for whom once was sufficient.

definition: Fierce, cruel, deadly; of a wound, blow, or creature: capable of killing; of a person or purpose: ruthlessly vicious.
lost: We kept fell in 'one fell swoop' — one of the most famous dead metaphors in English, carrying a word almost nobody can define. Fell meant deadly and it still does, if you know.
usage: He had a fell efficiency about him that made people very polite and very careful to give him no cause for complaint.

definition: A person's double or wraith; the apparition of a living person seen as an omen of their death; the self that appears before the self dies.
lost: Named the premonition as a specific visible form — a fetch is not a ghost but a preview, not the dead but the about-to-be-dead, which is a more unsettling category.
usage: She saw her own fetch in the mirror three days before the fever, which she told no one until afterward.

definition: Fated to die; having otherworldly second sight; marked by knowledge that comes from beyond the living world.
lost: We kept fey but stripped its prophetic weight — it now means whimsical, which is the exact opposite of what it once named.
usage: There was something fey about the way she described the flood three days before it came.

definition: Actually: a kilt — but used in phrase to mean to speak at tedious length, to delay by talking; from the garment associated with long Highland oratory.
lost: Worth including as a curiosity of folk etymology — filibuster comes from freebooter, not filibeg, but the association of the kilt with long-windedness persisted in dialect.
usage: He had been filibegging for forty minutes by the time the chairman called the vote.

definition: A polecat; a small, fierce mustelid of the hedgerows and warrens; also used as a term of abuse for a person of rank odour.
lost: Named the polecat with full awareness of its smell — fitchew is a word that carries the animal's chief characteristic in the sound of it.
usage: Something had taken three of the chickens in the night; the tracks and the smell confirmed a fitchew.

definition: Limp and heavy with moisture; of air: flat, oppressive, and still; weather that sits on you rather than moving.
lost: Named the specific misery of hot, damp, motionless air — flaggy is the weather of illness and inertia, heat without its compensation of movement.
usage: A flaggy August afternoon, the kind that made the horses irritable and the children fall asleep in unexpected places.

definition: A sudden raid into enemy territory; a brief incursion made to plunder rather than hold ground.
lost: Named the raid with precision — a foray is not an invasion or a battle but a fast, targeted strike, in and out before response is possible.
usage: They made a foray across the river before dawn and were back before the garrison knew they had gone.

definition: Utterly ruined or exhausted; done completely, past all recovery — not merely tired but finished; the state that follows the expenditure of the very last reserve.
lost: "Exhausted" is a condition. Fordone is a conclusion — the cost has been totaled and found to exceed what was available.
usage: They found him fordone at the boundary stone, unable to account for the preceding three days.

definition: The part of the day between sunrise and noon; the morning before midday; the first half of the day's work.
lost: Morning is loose; forenoon was precise — it named the exact half-day before noon with the implication that time was being measured and accounted for.
usage: The forenoon had been spent in preparation; by noon they had nothing to show for it but greater readiness.

definition: In truth; indeed — used to introduce a statement offered as simple fact, with the minor force of insisting that what follows is not debatable, not embellished, merely so.
lost: "Indeed" is affirmation. Forsooth is a small declaration — what follows is offered as truth without apology.
usage: "Forsooth, he was there before any of us arrived," said the innkeeper, and was believed.

definition: Utterly exhausted; spent completely to the very end of one's capacity, with nothing in reserve and no expectation of quick recovery.
lost: "Exhausted" is clinical. Forspent implies a reckoning — the full cost has been paid.
usage: She arrived forspent, and slept for three days without once speaking.

definition: Exhausted from hard physical labor; worn out by work; the body spent by sustained effort rather than deprivation.
lost: Named exhaustion by its specific cause — forswunk is the tiredness of someone who has worked, not merely the tiredness of someone who hasn't rested.
usage: By evening they were forswunk and filthy and too tired to speak, and the field was only half done.

definition: To wander until lost or exhausted; to wander beyond the point at which wandering is pleasurable.
lost: Named the far end of wandering — forwander is what happens when the journey goes on past its own sense, when the wanderer can no longer remember why they left.
usage: He had forwandered in the city for three hours before he admitted he did not know where he was.

definition: To repair a leak at sea by drawing a sail stuffed with oakum and rope beneath the hull until water pressure holds it against the hole; emergency surgery performed from outside on a moving vessel.
lost: Named a repair that required the sea itself as a clamp — to fother a ship is to use the water trying to kill you as the force that holds your solution in place, which is either ingenious or desperate depending on how fast you are sinking.
usage: They fothered her in four fathoms of water while the pumps ran, and she held long enough to reach the roads.

definition: The bioluminescent glow of decaying wood; cold light produced by fungus in rotting timber; light from something dying.
lost: Named the paradox of light from decay — foxfire is luminescence without heat, brightness without combustion, which made it seem both miraculous and sinister.
usage: The fallen log glowed faintly in the dark wood, foxfire tracing the lines of what the tree had been.

definition: The crash of something breaking; the loud noise of a thing coming apart.
lost: Named the sound of destruction specifically — fragor is what you hear when something that was whole is no longer.
usage: The fragor of the chandelier brought everyone from the adjoining rooms.

definition: A free landowner of the yeoman class; a man of property and standing below the nobility; the middle rank of English rural society.
lost: Named the class between serf and knight that did the actual work of organizing England — franklins kept the records, served on juries, and made the decisions that mattered at the parish level.
usage: The franklin sat below the knight at table and above the merchants, and was comfortable in both directions.

definition: To bind tightly; to pass ropes around a sail or a vessel to reinforce it; to lash something against the movement of the sea until it cannot work loose.
lost: Named the binding that argues with the ocean — to frap something is to impose human order on a situation the sea is trying to disorder, which is the whole of seamanship compressed into a syllable.
usage: They frapped the mast with whatever they had and prayed it would hold through the night, which it did, barely.

definition: A pirate or plunderer; one who fights for plunder rather than allegiance; a soldier whose loyalty is to whatever pays.
lost: Named the soldier who has dispensed with ideology — a freebooter serves whoever pays and takes whatever they can carry, which is war reduced to its economic essentials.
usage: The army that arrived was half regulars and half freebooters, and the difference was visible in how they behaved in the town.

definition: A sea mist rolling inland; a cold fog from the North Sea; the specific coastal mist that makes lighthouses necessary.
lost: Named the coastal fog by the sea it comes from — a fret is haar's near relation, the fog of fishing communities and lighthouse keepers, weather that knows the coast.
usage: A fret had come in off the sea by afternoon, reducing visibility to twenty yards and making the cliffs invisible from the beach.

definition: Frozen; frosty; of extreme and bitter cold; the poetic form of frozen that carries more of the cold's severity.
lost: Frozen describes a state; frore describes a quality — frore air is air that has become something other than air, hostile and crystalline.
usage: A frore morning, the kind where breath preceded you in a cloud and the puddles cracked underfoot like thin pottery.

definition: Brilliantly, dazzlingly shining; radiant with an intensity that demands acknowledgment.
lost: Distinguished intensity from mere presence — fulgent light is light that makes a claim on you.
usage: The fulgent face of the glacier turned every shadow blue.

definition: Of or pertaining to soot and smoke; dark, sooty; possessing the quality not of fire but of what fire leaves behind — residue, aftermath, the evidence of burning.
lost: "Smoky" describes the process. Fuliginous describes what remains after the process is complete.
usage: The ceiling was fuliginous with generations of candle-smoke and kept secrets well.

definition: Pompous, inflated, empty language; writing that mistakes weight for substance.
lost: Named a specific failure of prose — fustian is language that has dressed up without having anything to say.
usage: The report was pure fustian: twelve pages of impressive vocabulary covering an absence of conclusion.

definition: A wanderer, vagabond; one who cannot remain; a companion on the road.
lost: Named aimless travel as a character type, not a temporary condition — a gadling is constitutionally incapable of staying.
usage: She was a gadling by birth; every place she settled became a departure point within the year.

definition: To deny, contradict, or speak against something that has been declared — specifically to refuse what has been publicly stated, to stand against it with words.
lost: "Contradict" is clinical. Gainsay implies a public act — a refusal of what has been put forward, with all the consequence that entails.
usage: None who had seen it could gainsay the account, though it required believing difficult things.

definition: A quilted padded jacket worn under armour or alone; the poor soldier's defence; armour made of cloth and hope.
lost: Named the garment that most soldiers actually wore — not the gleaming knight but the foot soldier in quilted linen, which stopped less but was what most people had.
usage: The gambeson was soaked through by noon; fighting in wet padding was its own kind of torment.

definition: A wardrobe or small private room; also: the medieval toilet, a shaft in the castle wall — named for the theory that ammonia fumes preserved clothes.
lost: Named both the wardrobe and the toilet in one word because they were architecturally adjacent and functionally related — the etymology is either practical or optimistic depending on how you read it.
usage: The garderobe was positioned over the outer wall, which solved one problem and created another for anyone approaching from the east.

definition: Extravagant boasting; vainglorious bravado; the kind of boasting associated with Gascons, who were considered the most boastful people in France.
lost: Named boasting by its regional type — gasconade understood that there were grades of self-praise and the Gascon register was the highest, which the Gascons themselves might have agreed was a compliment.
usage: His account of the battle was pure gasconade; the men who had been there listened without expression until he finished.

definition: Originally: a spell that altered perception; a magical deception that made things appear other than they were; enchantment as visual distortion.
lost: The original glamour was a lie — a magical alteration of appearance, not natural beauty but manufactured illusion. The modern word kept the effect and forgot the mechanism.
usage: She had a glamour about her that made it difficult to remember, afterward, exactly what she had looked like.

definition: The power or practice of glamour; the witch's art of making things appear otherwise; enchantment wielded deliberately.
lost: The practitioner's word, as opposed to the effect — glamourie is what the enchanter does; glamour is what the enchanted see. The distinction between the art and the result.
usage: She was said to practise glamourie, which meant that no one was entirely sure what they saw when they looked at her.

definition: Land belonging to a parish church, worked to support the incumbent; also: any open meadow or cultivated soil.
lost: Named the land that sustained the priest — glebe was the ground that fed the institution, tying spirituality to soil in a way the modern church has abandoned.
usage: The glebe had not been ploughed in a generation; the living was too poor to attract anyone who cared.

definition: A kite; the soaring bird of prey that rides thermals above fields and carrion; also: a glowing coal.
lost: Named the bird and the ember together — both glede things rise and glow and consume, a metaphoric link the word carried without needing to explain.
usage: A glede hung above the harvested field all afternoon, reading the stubble for what the reapers had missed.

definition: A faint or guttering light; a candle or lantern barely holding against the dark. The word suggests not the source of light but its quality of imminent failure.
lost: "Candlelight" describes the source. Glim describes the quality of its failure.
usage: She navigated the passage by glim alone, the flame bending with each step.

definition: The period of twilight after sunset — not the moment of dusk but the slow interval that follows, when daylight has ended but darkness has not yet committed to anything.
lost: "Dusk" is the moment. Gloaming is the atmosphere — the quality of light that makes shapes uncertain and distances unreliable.
usage: By gloaming the roads were empty and the distances had become impossible to judge.

definition: A pool of standing water; a muddy puddle left by rain; the wet residue of weather that has passed.
lost: Named the puddle as a specific object — glogg is not abstract wetness but a particular standing water, a named thing you step around or into.
usage: The lane was a series of gloggs after three days of rain, and the horses picked their way through with the resignation of long experience.

definition: Originally: a dark or sullen sky; threatening cloud cover; the specific heaviness of light about to fail. The emotional sense came second.
lost: The weather meaning predates the emotional one by centuries — gloom was first something you looked up at, not something you felt, which gives the emotional usage a meteorological honesty.
usage: A gloom had been gathering since noon; by three it was dark enough to need a candle, and they had not thought to bring one.

definition: Direct spiritual knowledge received rather than learned; knowing as revelation, not study.
lost: Named the epistemological category that education cannot produce — the thing you suddenly know without having been taught.
usage: It was not something she could explain; it arrived as gnosis, complete and uninvited.

definition: An old grey she-cat; a familiar spirit in the form of a cat; a malicious or imperious old woman.
lost: Held the cat and the witch and the imperious elder together in one word — grimalkin understood that all three were variations of the same force.
usage: The grimalkin on the hearth regarded the new arrival with a contempt that seemed wholly considered.

definition: A shudder of horror or cold; the physical sensation of dread; to feel a creeping horror that expresses itself in the body before the mind has caught up.
lost: Named the shudder as an independent event — grue is the body's knowledge arriving before the thought, which is why 'gruesome' kept the root but lost the sensation.
usage: A grue ran through the group when the door opened; nothing was there, but the feeling persisted.

definition: A reward given for service rendered; recompense of a formal or ceremonial kind.
lost: Named reward as something earned and witnessed, not merely given — a guerdon is a public acknowledgment.
usage: He asked no guerdon for the crossing except the name of the man he carried.

definition: Fish offal, blood, and waste from the catch; the debris of the fishing boat's work; the smell and substance of what remains after the valuable has been taken.
lost: Named the residue of labour — gurry is what fishing leaves behind, the evidence of the work that the fish conceals. A boat smells of gurry; a fisherman smells of gurry; it is the signature of the trade that does not wash out.
usage: The quay was slick with gurry by the time the fleet came in, which was how you knew the catch had been good.

definition: A shackle or fetter, especially for the leg; to shackle; to bind at the ankle.
lost: Named the restraint that moved with you — gyves were not a wall or a cell but a weight you carried at every step.
usage: He walked with the careful gait of a man who had worn gyves and had not entirely forgotten the weight.

definition: A cold sea fog rolling inland from the North Sea; a damp coastal mist that arrives without announcement and stays.
lost: Named a specific meteorological event by its feel rather than its physics — haar is not merely fog but the particular cold intimacy of sea air coming ashore.
usage: A haar had settled over the harbour before dawn, and by morning the ships were invisible from the dock.

definition: Originally: the kingfisher, believed to nest on a calm sea; hence: a period of happiness, tranquillity, and perfect weather.
lost: Named the good time by the bird that supposedly caused it — halcyon days were the fourteen days of calm that the kingfisher required for her nest, granted by the gods mid-winter.
usage: A halcyon fortnight in late September that everyone later said had been the last good time before everything changed.

definition: Healthy and vigorous; robust in constitution; free from defect or infirmity — the hale body is the one that has not yet failed.
lost: We kept it in 'hale and hearty' but lost its standalone weight — hale was the medical term before medicine became technical, the word that meant nothing was wrong.
usage: He was hale at seventy in a way that made younger men feel obscurely criticised.

definition: Luck; fortune; what happens to one by chance; the unearned circumstance that shapes a life.
lost: Happy, happen, hapless, mishap — we kept all the derivatives but abandoned the root. Hap is luck understood as simply what falls, before we began to think of luck as something we might influence.
usage: He had arrived here by hap rather than design, which was true of most of the meaningful things in his life.

definition: Perhaps; by chance; if it so happen — a word that holds its uncertainty as a quality rather than an admission.
lost: Perhaps is neutral; haply carries the weight of what hap meant — the acknowledgment that chance governs more than we direct, that perhaps means not just maybe but maybe fate.
usage: Haply she would come, haply not; he prepared for both and tried to keep his face arranged for either.

definition: A knight's complete armour; the full equipment of a man-at-arms; also the gear of a working horse — the word served both.
lost: Named the equipment of war and labour in one word — harness understood that arming a knight and harnessing a horse were structurally the same act, dressing something powerful for use.
usage: He died in his harness, which was how he had always said he wanted to go, and nobody who knew him doubted it.

definition: Divination by the examination of animal entrails — specifically the liver; more broadly, any practice of reading the interior of things for knowledge that the surface does not offer.
lost: We kept the metaphor — "gut feeling" — and lost the word for the practice of taking it seriously.
usage: She had no patience for haruspicy but could not argue with the results.

definition: A coat of chainmail extending to the knees; the principal armour of the medieval knight before plate took over.
lost: Named the garment that made a man into a different kind of object — in a hauberk you moved differently, thought differently, were differently vulnerable.
usage: He had worn the hauberk so long he slept in it toward the end, and the rings left their pattern in his skin.

definition: The space between a ship's bow and her anchor cables; the pipes through which those cables run; the angle and arrangement of cables when a ship is riding to two anchors. A foul hawse is cables twisted around each other — a problem that requires patience or a knife.
lost: Named the ship's most forward anxiety — the hawse is where the vessel connects to the seabed, the point of attachment to something fixed in a world that is not fixed. A clean hawse meant a quiet night; a foul hawse meant work.
usage: The hawse was clear when they checked at midnight; by four the wind had veered and the cables had crossed, and the work of clearing them took until dawn.

definition: The floor of a fireplace; the fire itself as the centre of domestic life; home understood as a place with warmth at its middle.
lost: We kept hearth but lost its weight — it now means home in a sentimental sense, but it once meant the actual fire that cooked the food and heated the room and around which the family organised itself.
usage: The hearth had gone cold before dawn; whoever rose first would have to coax it back, which was understood without being said.

definition: Mental dullness; bluntness of feeling and perception; the state of having been worn smooth.
lost: Named dullness as a condition rather than a trait — hebetude is something that happens to you, not something you are.
usage: The months of paperwork had produced in him a thorough hebetude that no amount of sleep could shift.

definition: A journey made for safety from a dangerous situation; a flight or exodus; departure as survival.
lost: Named the departure that is not a choice — a hegira is forced travel, the journey you make because staying has become impossible.
usage: The family's hegira took three months and three countries and ended somewhere none of them had planned on.

definition: Before this time; up until the present moment — a word that draws a precise line between what existed in all the time before now, and the now that has changed it.
lost: "Previously" is neutral. Heretofore implies the present moment is a rupture — there was a before, and you are in the after.
usage: Nothing heretofore had prepared him for the specific quality of that silence.

definition: The remote inland areas beyond a coast or river; territory behind the known frontier; the land that lies beyond the edge of what is mapped.
lost: Named the space beyond the legible — the hinterland is not wilderness exactly but the place where the map's confidence runs out and inference begins.
usage: The hinterland remained officially unmapped for another generation, which meant it was officially inconvenient to govern.

definition: An untranslatable longing for a home — or a version of yourself — that no longer exists and perhaps never did exist quite as remembered.
lost: "Nostalgia" is medical in origin and implies a cure. Hiraeth implies none.
usage: The hiraeth came not for places but for the person she had been in them.

definition: A small, fast falcon used for hawking small birds and insects; a hawk quick enough to take swallows in flight.
lost: We kept hobby only as 'pastime' — the falcon sense, where the word came from, named a precision instrument of the air capable of things larger birds could not attempt.
usage: He kept a hobby for the larks, and a merlin for the skylark, and spent more on their upkeep than on his own.

definition: A formal, public act of allegiance; the ceremony by which a vassal acknowledged a lord's authority.
lost: Named the body's performance of loyalty — homage was not internal but enacted, witnessed, recorded.
usage: He paid homage without irony, which surprised everyone who thought they knew him.

definition: Nevertheless; however that may be; notwithstanding — a concession that holds its ground.
lost: Nevertheless is blunter; howbeit carried a note of philosophical resignation — however things may stand, here is what remains true.
usage: The evidence was thin, howbeit sufficient for the purposes of the court.

definition: The middle of the month in the Roman calendar; the fifteenth of March, May, July, and October; the thirteenth of all others — a day of reckoning.
lost: Named the month's midpoint as a fulcrum rather than just a date — the ides was the day everything turned around, and its weight has outlasted its calendar.
usage: He had borrowed the money on the ides and the ides had come again three times without repayment, which had become its own kind of answer.

definition: A spoken curse; the act of calling down evil or harm through deliberate utterance.
lost: Distinguished the curse as a performative speech act from the harm it invokes — an imprecation is a thing you do with your mouth.
usage: She left without violence, but the imprecation she murmured at the threshold was heard by three people.

definition: A series of words chanted or spoken as a magical spell; the use of voice as an instrument of power.
lost: Named speech that does rather than describes — an incantation is language used as action, words that change the state of the world rather than represent it.
usage: The incantation was in a language nobody present understood, which was considered essential to its working.

definition: Of the color of raw flesh; blood-red; to make red as blood.
lost: Held flesh and wound in the same word — incarnadine is the color of both beginnings and ends.
usage: The water was incarnadine by the time they reached the shore, and they understood what that meant.

definition: Embodied in flesh; existing in physical form; an abstraction given a body.
lost: Named the condition of having a body as a specific theological and philosophical state — to be incarnate is to have crossed a threshold.
usage: Standing there in the rain, she seemed to be grief incarnate, stripped of everything incidental.

definition: To compose or write; to set down in literary form; to dictate the terms of a written text.
lost: Named writing as a compositional act distinct from mere transcription — to indite is to give form, not just to record.
usage: She indited the letter herself, which was unusual, and the handwriting showed it.

definition: A formal ecclesiastical prohibition; the suspension of religious services from a community as punishment; a ban backed by spiritual authority.
lost: Named the ban that cost you heaven as well as standing — an interdict was not a legal prohibition but a religious one, denying sacraments to an entire community for the sins of its lord.
usage: The interdict had been in force for three years; the children born in that time were unbaptised, which the priest said would be addressed when the bishop lifted it.

definition: To speak or write against with great bitterness; to rail; to pour sustained verbal hostility onto a subject.
lost: Named criticism at the level of sustained attack — inveigh is not a complaint or a critique but a campaign, speech as siege.
usage: He inveighed against the proposal for forty minutes and convinced no one, which he had expected.

definition: To illuminate; to light up from within or without; to make radiant; to fill with light as if pouring it in.
lost: We kept irradiate but narrowed it to nuclear physics — the word once named any process of light-filling, the way a window irradiates a room in morning.
usage: The low sun irradiated the dust in the air and made the ordinary room look briefly miraculous.

definition: Traveling from place to place without fixed habitation; defined by motion rather than destination.
lost: Named the condition of permanent transit — an itinerant is not between places but has made transit itself the address.
usage: He had been itinerant so long that the question of where he lived produced a small, rehearsed hesitation.

definition: A small anchor used to move a ship by hand; to kedge is to carry the anchor out in a boat, drop it, then haul the ship toward it — progress measured in anchor lengths, patience as propulsion.
lost: Named movement achieved by repeated acts of anchoring — to kedge is to move without wind, using the seabed as a grip and human arms as the engine, which is slow and exhausting and sometimes the only option.
usage: They kedged her off the mud over four hours, the men in the boat laying the anchor ahead while those aboard hauled on the capstan.

definition: To wail in lamentation over the dead; a sustained, formal mourning cry; grief given a specific vocal form.
lost: Named grief at its most public — keening is not private weeping but communal, performative mourning, grief as a sound the living make for the dead so the dead know they are missed.
usage: The women keened from the doorway until the cart was out of sight, and then the silence was its own kind of sound.

definition: Knowing; the range of one's knowledge or sight; the distance at which something can be recognised — before it became a poetic compound.
lost: Named knowing as a distance — kenning was originally how far you could see and know, which is a spatial understanding of epistemology that the modern word has entirely abandoned.
usage: Beyond his kenning lay a stretch of coast he had heard described but never seen, and the descriptions had disagreed.

definition: Cows collectively; cattle; the archaic plural of cow, carrying the pastoral weight of a world organized around livestock.
lost: The loss of kine is the loss of a plural that felt like a multitude — cows is a count, kine was a collective presence.
usage: The kine moved across the valley floor in the evening light, slow and certain as weather.

definition: A woman's gown or outer petticoat; a man's tunic reaching to the knees; a garment that defined the body's public shape.
lost: Named a garment that no longer exists — the kirtle was a foundational layer of dress, neither underwear nor outerwear, with no modern equivalent.
usage: She had a kirtle of Lincoln green that she wore to market and kept folded the rest of the year.

definition: One's acquaintances, neighbors, and familiar people; one's home country and its people.
lost: We kept kith only in the phrase kith and kin, which no one says separately — kith alone named the looser circle of belonging, the people who know your name without being family.
usage: He had left his kith so long ago that returning felt less like homecoming than research.

definition: The sound of a bell rung slowly to announce a death or funeral; a sound that announces an ending.
lost: Named the bell's specific funerary voice — a knell is not ringing but tolling, not celebration but announcement, sound as public declaration of loss.
usage: The knell began before they reached the church, which meant the news had arrived before them.

definition: Softly bright; playing lightly over a surface without burning; light that moves rather than settles.
lost: Named a quality of light that 'glowing' misses — lambent light is alive, it moves across things rather than emanating from them.
usage: The lambent firelight played across the maps spread on the table.

definition: A pleasant or oppressive physical heaviness; the body slowed to a tempo just below waking.
lost: Named the ambiguous state where exhaustion and pleasure blur — languor is tiredness that might be enjoyable, and that ambiguity mattered.
usage: A summer languor settled over the house from noon until the sea wind came back at five.

definition: The archaic form of lantern, specifically as used aboard ship; the navigation light; also the poop lantern of a ship of the line, which identified the vessel at night and guided the fleet.
lost: The spelling preserves the material — lanthorns were originally made with panels of thinly scraped horn rather than glass, which was expensive and fragile. The horn let light through without shattering in the wind. The word kept the horn long after glass replaced it.
usage: The admiral's lanthorn burned all night, and the fleet steered by it, and when it was extinguished at dawn the lookouts knew to find another reference.

definition: Physical or mental weariness; exhaustion that goes deeper than sleep can reach.
lost: Distinguished exhaustion from tiredness — lassitude is not solved by rest, which is why it requires its own word.
usage: A lassitude had settled into her bones that she recognized as something other than lack of sleep.

definition: The sheltered side; the side away from the wind; the quarter toward which wind blows and where calm is found. A shore that is a lee shore is one you are being blown toward — comfort turned to danger.
lost: Named shelter as a directional fact — lee is not a place but a relationship, defined entirely by where the wind is coming from, which means it changes every time the wind does.
usage: They worked their way into the lee of the headland and anchored there for the night, the wind nothing but a sound above them.

definition: A physician or healer; one skilled in the medical arts; a doctor before that word carried its current authority.
lost: We kept leech only as the worm — lost the healer, kept the parasite, which says something about how medicine evolved.
usage: The leech came at dawn and spent an hour in silence before pronouncing, unhelpfully, that time would tell.

definition: A young hare in its first year; a hare still carrying the smallness and vulnerability of its first season.
lost: Named age in an animal with a precision we reserved for very few species — the leveret is a hare that hasn't yet become what it will be.
usage: She found a leveret in the long grass, close-huddled and convinced that stillness was invisibility.

definition: Gladly, willingly; with equal willingness — used in constructions like 'I had as lief' meaning 'I would just as gladly.' A way of stating preference as equivalence.
lost: Modern English has no clean equivalent for this construction. To say you would 'just as soon' is weaker; 'I had as lief die' cannot be translated without losing something.
usage: "I had as lief stay," he said, which was true, though no one believed him.

definition: One's rightful lord or sovereign; also: bound by feudal loyalty; owing or owed allegiance.
lost: Held both sides of the bond in one word — a liege was simultaneously the one owed loyalty and the one who owed protection.
usage: My liege, he said, and meant it; he had not served the man forty years in order to hedge the word now.

definition: A light, rhythmic musical quality; a rising and falling cadence in voice or music; the sound of something that moves with natural ease.
lost: Named the quality of music and voice that makes listening effortless — a lilt is rhythm you don't notice because it fits so naturally it feels like the shape of the air.
usage: She had a lilt in her voice that made everything she said sound like good news even when it wasn't.

definition: Of or relating to a threshold; occupying a position at or on both sides of a boundary; the state of being between.
lost: Named the threshold as a distinct category of place — liminal spaces are not inside or outside but the place where the transition is happening, which is a specific and disorienting location.
usage: The corridor had the liminal quality of all corridors: it was not where you came from and not where you were going and it did not pretend otherwise.

definition: Lithe and supple; bending easily; having the physical grace of something with no unnecessary stiffness.
lost: Named flexibility as a bodily quality, not merely a metaphor — lissom is about what a body can do without effort.
usage: She moved with a lissom ease through the rigging that made the new hands stare.

definition: Skill or cunning in the use and arrangement of words; verbal craft; the quality of a writer or speaker who shapes language with the same precision a carpenter gives to wood.
lost: "Wordsmith" is a metaphor of manufacture. Logodaedaly names the specific skill — the cunning, the architecture, the knowing use of the instrument.
usage: His logodaedaly was such that you only noticed, afterward, that you had been led.

definition: The glow of a lighthouse or lit coastline reflected on low cloud, visible over the horizon before the light source itself can be seen; the ghost of land before land appears.
lost: Named the precursor of arrival — to see the loom of a coast is to know land is there before you can prove it, a knowledge that arrives by inference and light-scatter. Sailors read the loom to navigate in darkness and fog.
usage: They saw the loom of the lightship two hours before they saw the light itself, which was enough to know they had not gone too far north.

definition: Calm, sheltered, and still; protected from the wind; of weather or a place: quiet and undisturbed.
lost: Named shelter not as a structure but as a quality — lown is the calmness that geography provides, the lee side of things.
usage: They found a lown hollow in the hillside and ate their bread there while the gale ran over the top of them.

definition: Glowing with soft light; translucent; emitting a quiet, interior luminescence.
lost: Glowing replaced it but dropped the translucency — lucent things glow from inside.
